section.title: Clean Garments for the High Priest
section.sectionRationale: The fourth night vision, and one of the most gospel-rich in the Old Testament. Joshua the high priest stands before the angel of the LORD in filthy garments while Satan accuses him. God rebukes the accuser, removes Joshua's iniquity, clothes him in clean robes, and announces the coming of His servant, the Branch, and the stone that will remove the iniquity of the land in a single day. This is justification in picture form.
section.thesis: The accused and defiled priest is not cast out but cleansed: God silences the accuser, removes his iniquity, clothes him in splendor, and points to the Branch and the stone through whom the sin of the land will be taken away in a single day.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Satan accusing the filthy-clothed Joshua and God rebuking him; the removal of Joshua's iniquity and his clothing in clean garments; and the charge to Joshua with the promise of the Branch, the stone with seven eyes, and iniquity removed in one day.

units.label: The accuser rebuked
units.note: a brand snatched from the fire

verses.text: Then the angel showed me Joshua the high priest standing before the angel of the LORD, with {{satan|Satan}} standing at his right hand to accuse him.
And the LORD said to Satan: “The LORD rebukes you, Satan! Indeed, the LORD, who has chosen Jerusalem, rebukes you! Is not this man a firebrand snatched from the fire?”
Now Joshua was dressed in filthy garments as he stood before the angel.
So the angel said to those standing before him, “Take off his {{tso'im|filthy}} clothes!” Then he said to Joshua, “See, I have removed your iniquity, and I will clothe you with splendid robes.”
Then I said, “Let them put a clean turban on his head.” So a clean turban was placed on his head, and they clothed him, as the angel of the LORD stood by.

units.label: The Branch and the stone
units.note: iniquity removed in a single day

verses.text: Then the angel of the LORD gave this charge to Joshua:

“This is what the LORD of Hosts says: ‘If you walk in My ways and keep My instructions, then you will govern My house and will also have charge of My courts; and I will give you a place among these who are standing here.
Hear now, O high priest Joshua, you and your companions seated before you, who are indeed a sign. For behold, I am going to bring My servant, the {{tsemach|Branch}}.

See the stone I have set before Joshua; on that one stone are seven eyes. Behold, I will engrave on it an inscription, declares the LORD of Hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day.
On that day, declares the LORD of Hosts, you will each invite your neighbor to sit under your own vine and fig tree.’”

satan.gloss: The accuser or adversary, literally the one who opposes in a legal sense, as at a trial. He stands at Joshua's right hand to press charges. The New Testament names him the accuser of the brethren who accuses them day and night, and shows him silenced by the blood of the Lamb.

tso'im.gloss: Filthy, soiled, the strongest word for defilement, used of excrement-stained garments. Joshua's filthy clothes picture the guilt and uncleanness of the priest and the people he represents. That God strips them off and reclothes him is a vivid picture of sin removed and righteousness given.

tsemach.gloss: A sprout, shoot, or branch that grows up from a stump or root. It becomes a messianic title for the coming Davidic King, the Branch who will build the temple and reign. Jeremiah calls Him the righteous Branch; Zechariah names Him My servant, the Branch, God's chosen priest-king.

basic.context: This is the fourth night vision, and it plays out like a courtroom scene. Joshua, the high priest of the returned exiles, stands before God in filthy clothes while Satan accuses him. But God rebukes the accuser, strips off the filthy garments, declares Joshua's sin removed, and clothes him in clean, splendid robes. Then God promises His servant the Branch and a stone through whom the land's sin will be removed in a single day.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a symbolic vision that teaches by picture. Joshua stands for the priesthood and the people, so his cleansing is theirs. People debate exactly what the stone with seven eyes is, but the main point is unmistakable: God justifies the guilty, silences their accuser, and points to the coming Branch, the Messiah.
basic.originalLanguages: The satan, the accuser, presses charges. Joshua wears tso'im clothes, the strongest word for filthy, stained garments picturing guilt. God announces His servant the tsemach, the Branch, a messianic title for the coming Davidic King, and promises to remove the land's iniquity in a single day.
basic.typology: This whole scene is a shadow of justification in Christ. The filthy garments removed and splendid robes given picture our sin taken away and Christ's righteousness put on. The Branch is the Messiah, the priest-king. Iniquity removed in a single day points to the one day at Calvary when sin was dealt with once for all.
basic.christ: Joshua's cleansing foreshadows what Christ does for every believer: our filthy garments exchanged for His righteousness. Satan the accuser is silenced not by Joshua's worth but by God's choice, just as we are cleared by the blood of the Lamb. The Branch is Jesus, the servant-King, and the single day of removed iniquity is the day of the cross.
basic.god: God is the righteous Judge who nonetheless justifies the guilty. He does not deny Joshua's filth; He removes it. He silences the accuser by grace, not by pretending there was no sin. He is holy, gracious, and set on cleansing and clothing His people through the coming Branch.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 61 speaks of garments of salvation and a robe of righteousness. Revelation 12 names Satan the accuser, silenced by the blood of the Lamb. Jeremiah 23 and 33 name the righteous Branch. Romans 8 asks who can bring a charge against God's elect, for it is God who justifies.
basic.oneStory: The fourth vision shows God justifying a guilty priest, silencing the accuser and clothing him in splendor, then pointing to the Branch who removes the land's sin in a single day. This is the gospel in shadow, fulfilled at the cross, and it carries the story forward to the day God's people stand clean before Him.

context.literary: The fourth and central-turning vision, distinct in form: instead of Zechariah asking questions, he watches a courtroom drama unfold, then hears an oracle. It pairs closely with the fifth vision of the lampstand in chapter 4, together addressing the two leaders of the restoration, Joshua the priest and Zerubbabel the governor. The vision moves from accusation to cleansing to the promise of the Branch, deepening from a personal pardon to the removal of the whole land's iniquity.
context.historical: Joshua son of Jehozadak was the actual high priest of the returned community, serving alongside the governor Zerubbabel as the temple was being rebuilt. A priesthood freshly emerged from exile, ministering in a half-built temple, might well feel unworthy and defiled, and hostile neighbors were accusing the community before Persian authorities. The vision assures the priest and people that God has cleansed them for service.
context.canonical: The scene draws on the Day of Atonement imagery of the high priest, filthy garments, and cleansing, and on the courtroom picture of the accuser found in Job and echoed in Revelation. The Branch title links back to Isaiah and Jeremiah and forward to Zechariah 6, where the Branch is crowned as priest-king. The removal of iniquity in a single day anticipates the once-for-all atonement of Hebrews.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure the high priest and the whole community, through this courtroom vision, that God has silenced their accuser, removed their guilt, and clothed them for holy service, and to fix their hope on the coming servant, the Branch, and the day when God will remove the land's iniquity once for all.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The vision describes God's gracious act of cleansing and His promise of the Branch; the condition in verse 7, if you walk in My ways, is a real call to faithful obedience that flows from the cleansing already given. The abiding truths are justification by God's grace, the silencing of the accuser, and the coming of the Messiah.

debates.question: What is the stone with seven eyes set before Joshua?

views.label: A messianic symbol tied to the Branch and the cornerstone
views.case: The stone stands alongside the Branch as a picture of the Messiah, the tested cornerstone of Isaiah 28 and the stone the builders rejected, on whom God's full sight, the seven eyes, rests.

views.label: A symbol of the temple, the community, or God's watchful care
views.case: Others read the stone as the foundation or capstone of the rebuilt temple, or the seven eyes as God's all-seeing providence over the work, later linked to the seven eyes that scan the whole earth in 4:10.

debates.bearing: Whatever the precise referent, the stone is bound to the promise of iniquity removed in a single day, so its meaning centers on God's provision for cleansing, which the New Testament sees fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: How does the condition if you walk in My ways relate to the free cleansing already given?

views.label: Obedience flows from grace, not the reverse
views.case: God removes Joshua's iniquity and clothes him first, unconditionally; the charge to walk faithfully is the fruit and privilege of that cleansing, granting responsibility in God's house.

views.label: Continued privilege depends on faithful service
views.case: The specific privileges of governing God's house and having access among the heavenly attendants are held out as things to be entered by ongoing obedience.

debates.bearing: Both agree the cleansing itself is God's free gift; the debate concerns the role of the subsequent obedience, which Scripture treats as the response to grace, not its price.

study.originalLanguages: The vision unfolds as a trial and turns on three words. At Joshua's right hand stands the satan, the accuser or adversary, the legal opponent who presses charges, later named the accuser of the brethren. Joshua is clothed in tso'im garments, the strongest Hebrew word for filthy, used of excrement-stained cloth, a stark picture of the guilt of priest and people. God does not dispute the filth; He removes it, declaring, I have removed your iniquity, and clothes Joshua in splendor. Then the oracle names the hope: God will bring His servant the tsemach, the Branch, the sprout from David's stump, a messianic title for the coming priest-king who will build the temple and reign. Bound to Him is the promise to remove the iniquity of this land in a single day, the one day the New Testament finds at the cross.

typology.type: Filthy garments removed and splendid robes given
typology.fulfillment: Justification in Christ, our sin taken away and His righteousness put on, Isaiah 61:10 and 2 Corinthians 5:21.

typology.type: The accuser silenced by God's choice, not Joshua's merit
typology.fulfillment: Satan the accuser overcome by the blood of the Lamb, so no charge stands against God's elect, Revelation 12:10-11 and Romans 8:33-34.

typology.type: My servant, the Branch, and iniquity removed in a single day
typology.fulfillment: Christ the Davidic priest-king who removes sin once for all on the day of the cross, Jeremiah 23:5-6 and Hebrews 10:10-14.

study.christ: Few Old Testament scenes preach the gospel as clearly as this one. Joshua stands guilty and defiled, the accuser at his side, and by every measure of justice he should be condemned. Instead God rebukes the accuser, strips off the filthy garments, and clothes him in splendor, saying, I have removed your iniquity. This is justification in picture: the guilty declared clean, not because the charges were false but because God Himself takes the guilt away and gives righteousness in its place, exactly what Christ accomplishes for the believer, who is made the righteousness of God in Him. The accuser is silenced not by Joshua's worth but by God's electing choice of Jerusalem, just as Satan is finally overcome by the blood of the Lamb. And the oracle names the one through whom it all holds: My servant, the Branch, the Davidic priest-king, and the promise to remove the iniquity of the land in a single day, fulfilled on the single day of Calvary when Christ put away sin once for all.
study.god: This vision reveals God as the righteous Judge who justifies the ungodly. He is holy, and He does not pretend the filth away; the garments really are defiled, the sin really is there. Yet He is gracious beyond reckoning: He takes the initiative to silence the accuser, to remove the iniquity, and to clothe the guilty in splendor, all on the ground of His own choice rather than the priest's merit. He is a God who provides the very cleansing He requires, who fixes His people's hope on His coming servant the Branch, and who plans to deal with sin decisively and finally in a single day. Justice and mercy meet in Him, and the outcome is a cleansed people restored to holy service.

crossReferences.note: God clothes His people with garments of salvation and a robe of righteousness, the reality behind Joshua's splendid robes.
Satan the accuser is thrown down and overcome by the blood of the Lamb, fulfilling the LORD rebukes you, Satan.
The LORD will raise up for David a righteous Branch who will reign, the messianic title Zechariah uses here.
By a single offering Christ has perfected for all time those being sanctified, fulfilling iniquity removed in a single day.

study.oneStory: The fourth night vision stages the gospel centuries before the cross: a guilty priest, an accuser, and a holy God who removes the filth, silences the charge, clothes the sinner in splendor, and promises to take away the land's iniquity in a single day through His servant the Branch. The scene runs straight to Christ, in whom our filthy garments are exchanged for His righteousness and sin is dealt with once for all at Calvary, carrying the story on toward the day God's cleansed people rest secure under vine and fig tree.

quiz.q: How does God respond to Satan's accusation of Joshua?

options.text: He rebukes the accuser and points to His own choice of Jerusalem, calling Joshua a brand snatched from the fire.
options.note: Verse 2 shows God silencing the accuser on the ground of His grace, not Joshua's merit.

options.text: He agrees with Satan and condemns Joshua.
options.note: The filth is real, but God removes it rather than condemning; He rebukes the accuser.

options.text: He ignores the accusation entirely.
options.note: God directly answers and rebukes the accuser rather than ignoring him.

options.text: He tells Joshua to defend himself.
options.note: Joshua says nothing; God Himself acts to cleanse and defend him.

quiz.q: What do the filthy garments and clean robes picture?

options.text: Sin removed and righteousness given, a picture of justification.
options.note: Verse 4 shows iniquity taken away and splendid robes given, the gospel in shadow.

options.text: A change of ordinary work clothes.
options.note: The garments are symbolic of guilt and righteousness, not everyday attire.

options.text: That Joshua was being demoted from the priesthood.
options.note: He is cleansed and restored to service, not demoted.

options.text: That appearances do not matter to God.
options.note: The garments matter precisely because they picture spiritual guilt and cleansing.

quiz.q: Who is My servant, the Branch?

options.text: The coming messianic priest-king, fulfilled in Jesus Christ.
options.note: The Branch is a Davidic messianic title, fulfilled in Christ the servant-King.

options.text: Simply another name for Joshua himself.
options.note: Joshua is a sign pointing to the Branch, not the Branch himself.

options.text: The rebuilt temple building.
options.note: The Branch is a person, God's servant, who will build the temple.

options.text: The Persian king Darius.
options.note: The Branch is the promised Davidic Messiah, not a pagan king.

quiz.q: What does I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day point to?

options.text: The once-for-all removal of sin accomplished by Christ at the cross.
options.note: The single day anticipates Calvary, where sin was dealt with once for all.

options.text: A single festival day of the year with no lasting effect.
options.note: It points beyond any repeated ritual to a decisive, final removal of sin.

options.text: That sin can never really be removed.
options.note: The promise is exactly that iniquity will be removed, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: A day when the people would cleanse themselves.
options.note: God says I will remove the iniquity; the cleansing is His work.

quiz.q: What does this vision reveal about God?

options.text: He is the righteous Judge who justifies the guilty by removing their sin and clothing them in righteousness.
options.note: God does not deny the filth but takes it away, joining justice and grace.

options.text: He overlooks sin as if it did not matter.
options.note: He does not overlook the filth; He removes it, which honors both justice and mercy.

options.text: He justifies people on the basis of their own merit.
options.note: Joshua is cleansed by God's choice and grace, not his own worth.

options.text: He sides with the accuser against His people.
options.note: He rebukes the accuser and defends His chosen people.

questions: Joshua stood guilty and silent while God removed his filth and clothed him. Where do you need to stop defending yourself and simply receive God's cleansing?
The accuser was silenced by God's choice, not Joshua's worth. When accusing voices, from Satan or your own conscience, condemn you, how does the blood of Christ answer them?
God exchanged filthy garments for splendid robes. What would it mean to live today clothed in Christ's righteousness rather than your own performance?
The Branch, the servant-King, was the community's hope. How does fixing your eyes on Jesus the priest-king steady you when you feel unworthy to serve?
God promised to remove iniquity in a single day, fulfilled at the cross. How does the finished, once-for-all work of Christ free you from trying to earn your standing with God?

translationNotes.note: Satan renders the Hebrew satan, the accuser or adversary; a footnote gives that is, the Accuser or the Adversary. He stands at the right hand, the accuser's place in a trial.
Filthy renders tso'im, the strongest word for defilement, used of excrement-stained clothing; the removal pictures the taking away of guilt.
Branch renders tsemach, a sprout or shoot; it is a messianic title for the coming Davidic priest-king, developed in Jeremiah 23:5, 33:15 and Zechariah 6:12.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 3:1-10. All 10 verses reproduced in order. No em dashes present in this passage.
